sector: Nuclear / Small Modular Reactors (clean-firm power generation for AI data-center load)
bull_thesis: NuScale is a binary, option-like bet on the single hardest physical bottleneck in the AI build-out: clean, firm, around-the-clock baseload power that hyperscalers and sovereign-AI projects must secure to run gigawatt-scale data centers. Its core asset is genuinely scarce: it holds the only design certified by the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) for a small modular reactor (SMR — a factory-built nuclear unit far smaller than a conventional plant), in 50-megawatt-electric modules uprated to 77 MWe. Competitors are still years behind on that regulatory approval. The load-bearing catalyst is the September 2025 TVA and ENTRA1 program for up to 6 gigawatts of NuScale SMRs — potentially the largest U.S. nuclear deployment in history — with management guiding a definitive power-purchase agreement (PPA, a long-term contract to sell electricity at a fixed price) by year-end 2026 and an equipment-supply contract immediately after. The CFO guided that signed milestone payments from those contracts would exceed remaining engineering obligations, flipping NuScale operationally cash-flow-positive by end of 2026. The balance sheet is solid enough to wait: roughly $1.2 billion in liquidity against a development-stage burn rate, so there is no near-term solvency clock. NuScale also sidesteps the high-assay low-enriched uranium (HALEU — a specialty fuel not yet commercially available at scale) bottleneck that constrains some rival designs, because it runs on standard low-enriched uranium (LEU) supplied by Framatome under a 444-assembly agreement, and Doosan Enerbility is already manufacturing components — a live, de-risked supply chain. RoPower in Romania, covering six modules with pre-project financing approved in February 2026, is a second path to first revenue. If even one PPA plus equipment contract signs, NuScale re-rates from a pre-revenue option to the first commercial SMR vendor with a multi-gigawatt backlog, and the AI-power demand wave — estimated at over $725 billion in 2026 capital spending — gives it years of durable runway.
bear_thesis: NuScale is a pre-revenue, cash-burning technology developer that has never sold a commercial reactor, yet is priced as if it already had. Trailing-twelve-month (TTM — the most recent four quarters combined) revenue is $18.7 million, down 62% year over year. Revenue in the first quarter of 2026 was $0.6 million versus $13.4 million in the same quarter a year earlier. TTM net loss is negative $385.8 million, and Q1 2026 alone consumed $314.7 million of operating cash. At a roughly $4.3 billion market capitalization, that works out to approximately 85 times forward sales for a company whose entire value rests on an unsigned, third-party, serially-implied 'by year-end' power-purchase agreement — the exact pattern that already destroyed this company once: the UAMPS Carbon Free Power Project flagship was cancelled in November 2023 when subscription fell short and cost estimates climbed past $89 per megawatt-hour. The cash-flow-positive-by-year-end-2026 guidance is not operational; it is contingent on a contract that TVA and ENTRA1 — not NuScale — control, and management itself conceded the process is 'complicated and slow.' NuScale's economics inside the 6-gigawatt program are a derivative of intermediary ENTRA1's deal structure, not a direct contract, making value capture uncertain. The competitive moat is narrowing: TVA's actually-breaking-ground SMR at Clinch River uses GE-Hitachi's BWRX-300 design, not NuScale's; GE Vernova, Oklo, X-energy, and Holtec are all advancing through the regulatory pipeline. Founding sponsor Fluor exited its equity stake — a quiet signal about insider conviction at current prices. Liquidity is real but was built through share dilution, with 365 million-plus shares outstanding and climbing. A PPA slip into 2027 would force another equity raise at a far lower price. The stock already ran from single digits to $57 and round-tripped to roughly $11; the catalyst is largely a hope embedded in a still-rich multiple, and the thesis shares the same single demand driver — AI capital-spending durability — as the rest of the basket.
sop_bull: Roughly $18 to $22 per share. NuScale has no separate business segments to add up; it is a single pre-revenue option, so the right tool is option valuation, not a discounted cash-flow model. Net cash and liquidity of approximately $1.2 billion works out to about $3.30 per share as a hard floor. The 6-gigawatt TVA and ENTRA1 program, if it converts to a signed power-purchase agreement plus equipment contract, plausibly supports a multi-gigawatt backlog. Assigning a risk-adjusted enterprise value of roughly $5 to $6 billion to first-commercial-vendor status — a small fraction of the implied lifetime program value, weighted at the high end for a signed PPA — and adding back the $1.2 billion in cash yields approximately $18 to $22 per share. This is an option-value figure, not an intrinsic number; it assumes the PPA fires and that NuScale captures real economics inside the ENTRA1 structure.
sop_bear: Roughly $3 to $5 per share. Strip the unsigned catalyst entirely and value what is verifiable today: net cash and liquidity of approximately $1.2 billion (about $3.30 per share) is the hard floor, plus a modest premium for the NRC-certified design as a strategic or acquisition asset — roughly $0.5 to $1.5 billion, because certification is real but generates no revenue and competitors are closing the gap. TTM sales of $18.7 million support essentially no operating value on their own. The range is approximately $3 to $5 per share. A PPA slip into 2027 combined with further share dilution pushes toward the low end and potentially below.
sop_breakdown: Net cash and liquidity of approximately $1.2 billion works out to about $3.30 per share and acts as the hard floor. The NRC-certified design, valued as a strategic option and probability-weighted across a PPA-signs versus PPA-slips scenario, adds roughly $1.5 to $2.0 billion on a risk-adjusted basis, or about $4 to $5.50 per share. Pre-revenue operations contribute nothing. The base-case midpoint is approximately $6 to $8 per share, meaning the current price of roughly $11.70 already embeds a meaningful probability that the TVA power-purchase agreement signs. That leaves limited margin of safety (the gap between price and intrinsic value) and asymmetric downside if the deal slips.
risk_reward: Poor-to-balanced and binary. On the downside, a move from roughly $11.70 to the $3.30 cash floor — or to the roughly $6 risk-adjusted base — represents 40 to 70% downside if the PPA slips and dilution resumes. On the upside, the bull option case of $18 to $22 represents 55 to 90% upside if the PPA signs. On a probability-weighted basis the two sides are roughly equal, but the left tail (the bad outcome) is fat: the market already paid for much of the optionality after the 2025 rally, so the asymmetry that existed before the TVA announcement is largely spent.
peer_comps_note: True peers within the power-generation basket, with live multiples verified June 18 to 22, 2026. Oklo (ticker OKLO): market cap approximately $8.7 to $11 billion, roughly $61 per share. Also pre-revenue SMR but not yet NRC-certified; uses HALEU fuel with design certification still pending. Even more speculative and even more expensively priced than NuScale. GE Vernova (ticker GEV): market cap approximately $290 billion, forward price-to-earnings (share price divided by expected annual earnings per share) of roughly 57 times. Profitable, with multi-billion-dollar quarterly revenue; it is building the BWRX-300, the design TVA is actually deploying first at Clinch River. GEV is the established, de-risked nuclear and grid play. Constellation Energy (ticker CEG): a profitable nuclear operator and the cash-generating incumbent. Ranking: on valuation, NuScale is the most stretched on a sales basis — roughly 85 times forward price-to-sales (share price divided by expected revenue per share) versus roughly 4 times for GEV — and only marginally less speculative than OKLO. On revenue growth, NuScale's reported revenue is declining (down 62% TTM) while GEV grows. On margins and returns, NuScale and OKLO are deeply loss-making while GEV and CEG are profitable. On AI capital-spending exposure, all four are levered to data-center power demand, but NuScale and OKLO are the pure-option, highest-volatility expressions while GEV and CEG are the lower-risk ways to own the same demand theme. NuScale's single differentiator versus OKLO is NRC certification and standard-LEU fuel with no HALEU bottleneck. Its key competitive vulnerability is that the first U.S. utility SMR deployment — TVA's Clinch River site — uses a competitor's reactor.
role_in_scaleout: NuScale sits in the power-generation layer — the clean, firm baseload bottleneck that every AI capital-spending dollar must ultimately pay to energize gigawatt-scale data centers. Its specific role is as the only NRC-certified SMR vendor: the first-mover option on commercial small-modular nuclear for hyperscaler and sovereign-AI load. That demand is durable and secular, but NuScale's capture of it is not yet proven. It has zero signed commercial reactor orders, so demand durability is high while this name's claim on it remains an unvalidated option.
consensus_delta: Where we disagree with the market: the street is not clearly looking the wrong way here. Analyst consensus is already Hold across 17 analysts with a price target of roughly $15, and the stock has already round-tripped from $57 to roughly $11, so institutional sentiment is appropriately skeptical even as momentum and retail buying drove the 2025 spike. The real expectations gap sits in the optimists' assumption: both the bull case and management treat 'PPA plus equipment contract by year-end 2026, then cash-flow-positive' as near-certain, when it is a non-binding, third-party-controlled 'later this year' — the same serially-slipping pattern that already failed once at this company (the UAMPS project, cancelled 2023). The factually shaky assumption is that NuScale captures the full economics of the 6-gigawatt TVA program; in reality the customer relationship and equipment economics sit with intermediary ENTRA1, and TVA's first actual SMR deployment at Clinch River uses GE-Hitachi's BWRX-300 design, not NuScale's. There is no hidden mispricing to arbitrage; the roughly 85 times forward sales multiple already prices in a substantial probability that the PPA succeeds.
valley_of_death: The risky window spans roughly the next 3 to 9 months, during which concrete downside could materialize before any catalyst can crystallize. First, cash burn: Q1 2026 consumed $314.7 million of operating cash (driven by working-capital movements) and the company runs structural operating losses of roughly $57.5 million per quarter. The $1.2 billion liquidity cushion is ample but is being spent, and every quarter without a signed contract is dilution risk. Second, share dilution overhang: liquidity was rebuilt through at-the-market (ATM) share offerings — a mechanism where new shares are sold gradually into the open market — and Fluor's recent equity exit removes a strategic backstop. A further PPA delay toward 2027 would force NuScale to raise capital again at a far lower price. Third, catalyst-slip risk: management already hedged with the phrase 'complicated and slow'; any admission on the Q2 or Q3 earnings call that the PPA will not close this year would puncture the cash-flow-positive narrative and re-rate the stock toward the cash floor. Fourth, macro: the entire thesis depends on AI capital-spending durability, and any hyperscaler spending guide-down would hit this high-beta (2.22 beta means the stock moves roughly twice as much as the broader market) name hardest.
positioning_washout: This is a momentum- and retail-heavy, high-beta name (beta 2.22) that already experienced a forced-selling episode — collapsing from $57 to a low of roughly $8.85 before recovering to roughly $11.70. There is no dividend-yield base or passive-income investor cohort to trigger a mechanical forced liquidation, so the selling dynamic differs from a typical value-stock washout. Instead, NuScale is a crowded momentum long whose holders are catalyst-tourists. A disappointing PPA-timing comment would trigger momentum unwind and stop-loss selling rather than a forced yield-investor exit. The prior price collapse was real, but the current shareholder base is still speculative-long rather than capitulated to value-buyer hands, meaning the stock can wash out further on a catalyst slip.
forcing_function: The hard catalyst that could force a re-rating is the TVA and ENTRA1 definitive power-purchase agreement, guided 'by year-end 2026,' with the equipment-supply contract immediately after as the cash-flow-positive trigger. A secondary forcing event would be progress on the RoPower scope and contract in Romania. The Q2 2026 and Q3 2026 earnings calls are the dated checkpoints where management must either confirm the PPA is imminent or admit a slip. There is no binding contractual deadline — this catalyst is soft and serially extendable, which is the central risk.
moderator_conclusion: Applying the current regime context — late cycle, fully invested, AI capital spending as the single load-bearing factor, with a framework that demands each book hold names with independent return drivers — SMR is exactly the name the framework penalizes. Its load-bearing catalyst is dated but soft with no contractual payout, a non-binding, third-party 'by year-end' power-purchase agreement of the same archetype that already failed once at this company in 2023, so it earns no favorable-window credit. Its thesis hinges on the same single macro factor — AI capital-spending durability — as the rest of the power-generation basket, violating the requirement for independent return drivers across positions. Much of the optionality is already priced in: the stock ran to $57 and, even at roughly $11.70, trades at approximately 85 times forward sales on declining revenue, leaving a thin margin of safety against a roughly $3.30 cash floor and roughly $6 to $8 base sum-of-the-parts (valuing each asset separately and adding them up). The genuine positives — sole NRC certification, standard-LEU fuel supply chain with no HALEU bottleneck, $1.2 billion in liquidity, a real 6-gigawatt counterparty in TVA and ENTRA1 — make NuScale a credible speculative option, not an investable position at this price. Verdict: pass for now, conviction 2 of 5. Value conviction likewise 2: stripped of the catalyst, you are paying roughly $11.70 for a roughly $6 to $8 base sum-of-the-parts on a pre-revenue, cash-burning developer with an eroding regulatory edge. If one wanted exposure, the watchlist trigger for wait for the wash-out would be either a flush to the cash floor (roughly $4 to $6) or a genuinely binding, signed PPA and equipment contract — either event would change the risk-reward; today neither exists.
interrogator_dossier:
Interrogator Forensic Dossier: NuScale Power Corp (NYSE: SMR)

Prepared for the investment committee. As of 2026-06-22. Price approximately $11.70; market cap approximately $4.3 billion; 365 million shares. Pre-revenue, pre-commercial-sale nuclear technology developer. Scale-out power-generation basket — the only NRC-certified SMR design.

1. Narrative Arc

NuScale's story has run through three distinct chapters.

Chapter 1 (2023 to early 2024): the UAMPS Carbon Free Power Project (CFPP) was the entire thesis. That project was cancelled in November 2023 when subscription fell short and escalating cost estimates ($89 per megawatt-hour and rising) made it uneconomic. The stock collapsed and the original narrative was effectively destroyed.

Chapter 2 (2024): survival and reset. Management pivoted hard to cost-cutting — a roughly 28% workforce reduction in early 2024 — preserving cash and re-marketing the NRC-certified design to data-center and hyperscaler-adjacent demand. The narrative shifted from 'we have a flagship customer' to 'we have the only certified technology and the AI-power wave is coming to us.'

Chapter 3 (late 2025 to 2026, current): the AI capital-spending re-rating. The September 2025 TVA and ENTRA1 announcement — up to 6 gigawatts of NuScale SMRs, potentially the largest U.S. nuclear program ever — re-ignited the stock, which ran from single digits to a $57 high. The current narrative is entirely forward-looking: a TVA power-purchase agreement expected by year-end 2026, an equipment-supply contract to follow that flips the company operationally cash-flow-positive by end of 2026, plus RoPower (Romania, 6 modules) and a Framatome fuel supply chain. The story today is fully catalyst-dependent and zero percent revenue-validated. TTM revenue is $18.7 million, down 62% year over year, and the company has never sold a commercial reactor.

2. Claims vs. Financials (Forensic)

Management claim: 'Advancing well,' 'mega gigawatts' of demand, 6-gigawatt TVA program. The financials: TTM revenue $18.7 million, down 61.9%; Q1 2026 revenue $0.6 million versus $13.4 million in the prior year. Assessment: The narrative is massively richer than the numbers. None of that demand has converted to a signed power-purchase agreement or binding order.

Management claim: 'Operationally cash-flow-positive by end of 2026' (CFO Hamady). The financials: Q1 2026 operating cash outflow $314.7 million; operating loss $57.5 million; net loss $46.7 million; TTM net loss negative $385.8 million. Assessment: A heroic gap. The cash-flow-positive outcome depends entirely on an unsigned equipment-supply contract whose milestone payments would exceed remaining program-management-and-administration (PMA) obligations. The target is contingent, not operational.

Management claim: '$1.2 billion war chest,' strong liquidity. Reality: Liquidity is real (roughly $1.0 billion at Q1, roughly $1.2 billion by May), but it was built through dilutive at-the-market share offerings and Fluor's equity exit. Assessment: True, but the source is dilution. With 365 million shares and climbing, the balance sheet is funded by selling stock into the AI-nuclear excitement, not by operations.

Management claim: 'No fuel bottlenecks — we got ahead of it' (Framatome, standard LEU not HALEU). The financials: 444 fuel-assembly order placed; standard low-enriched uranium design confirmed. Assessment: A credible positive — it sidesteps the HALEU constraint that hampers some rival designs. A genuine differentiator, though pre-revenue.

Management claim: Only NRC-certified SMR design. Reality: True — certified design (50 MWe, uprated to 77 MWe per module). Assessment: True, and the single hardest competitive asset. But certification is not a built or sold plant.

The forensic core: every operational metric is deteriorating (revenue down, losses widening, cash burning roughly $300 million per quarter on working-capital swings) while the narrative is at an all-time high of ambition. The financials justify a development-stage option, not a commercial enterprise valuation.

3. Tone and Confidence Trajectory

Rising and aggressively optimistic — and increasingly specific in a way that creates accountability risk. CEO Hopkins described discussions as 'advancing well.' CFO Hamady put a hard stake in the ground: 'I hope to be operationally cash flow positive by the end of this year' — far more specific guidance than the cautious 2024 survival-mode tone. The shift is from defensive (cost-cuts, cash preservation in 2024) back to offensive (capacity, demand, cash-flow-positive target). But Hopkins also conceded the process is 'complicated and slow' — a tell that management knows the timeline risk. Tone is confident; the confidence is unbacked by signed contracts.

4. Guidance Credibility

Low to medium. The company's single most important historical guidance event — the CFPP/UAMPS flagship — failed outright (cancelled 2023). That is the defining credibility scar: management's last major 'this is happening' deal did not happen. Since then there has been no commercial revenue to beat or miss. The current cash-flow-positive-by-year-end-2026 and TVA-PPA-by-year-end-2026 guidance are untested promises riding on third-party decisions (TVA, ENTRA1) outside NuScale's control. Reliability score: low until a binding PPA or equipment contract is actually signed.

5. Analyst Pressure Points

Analysts pushed directly on slow near-term adoption; Hopkins admitted it is 'complicated and slow.' The recurring deflection is timing certainty: management answers 'later this year' or 'by year-end' without committing to a dated, binding milestone. Analysts also probe the scope and revenue potential at RoPower — and management openly conceded 'we're still working through what that scope actually is.' The unaddressed worry: what happens to the cash-flow-positive thesis if the TVA power-purchase agreement slips into 2027, which would force more dilution at a lower stock price. Consensus rating is Hold (17 analysts), price target roughly $15 — the street is skeptical, not euphoric, at the analyst level even as retail and momentum drove the stock.

6. Risk Flags and Positive Signals

Key concerns: Q1 2026 revenue was $0.6 million, down from $13.4 million in the prior year — optically catastrophic for a 'ramping' story. The CFPP/UAMPS flagship was cancelled in 2023 — the only prior commercial deal failed. Liquidity was rebuilt through at-the-market dilution; 365 million-plus shares and rising; the cash-flow-positive claim hinges on an unsigned contract. TTM net loss is negative $385.8 million; cash burn exceeded $300 million in a single quarter. Beta is 2.22 — a high-volatility momentum vehicle, not a defensive utility.

Positive signals: NuScale holds the only NRC-certified SMR design — a real, hard, multi-year regulatory asset. Standard low-enriched uranium fuel via Framatome sidesteps the HALEU bottleneck, and the supply chain was de-risked early. The TVA and ENTRA1 6-gigawatt program is a genuine, named, large counterparty; if a power-purchase agreement signs, it would be transformational. Doosan Enerbility is already manufacturing components — proof the supply chain is live.

7. Hidden Signals

The terminology shift from 'customer' to 'potential customers seeking mega gigawatts' and 'engagement' is the tell of a pre-contract pipeline dressed as demand. The reliance on third-party intermediary ENTRA1 — which holds the customer relationship and equipment economics — means NuScale's economics in the 6-gigawatt program are a derivative of ENTRA1's deal structure, not a directly contracted inflow. A subtle but material point about who captures value. Fluor — NuScale's longtime strategic backer — exited its equity stake; management framed this as providing liquidity, but a founding-sponsor exit at this stage is a quiet negative signal about insider conviction at current prices.

8. Capital-Allocation Assessment (for the director)

Trajectory: turning. NuScale is a pre-commercial technology developer whose entire value rests on converting an NRC-certified design and an AI-power demand wave into the first signed commercial SMR order. It has pivoted from a failed flagship to a hyperscaler-adjacent demand thesis but has not yet validated a single dollar of commercial reactor revenue.

The single most important thing the director must weigh: the TVA and ENTRA1 power-purchase agreement is a third-party, non-binding, serially-implied 'by year-end' catalyst — the same archetype (a slipping 'later this year' deal) that already burned this company once (the CFPP project, 2023). Position sizing must assume the PPA can slip into 2027, forcing dilution at a lower price.

Moat: the NRC-certified design and first-mover regulatory position constitute a regulatory-license moat — genuinely real and hard to replicate, but narrow. It protects the option, not an earnings stream. Competitors including GE-Hitachi (with the BWRX-300 already breaking ground at TVA's Clinch River site), Oklo, X-energy, and Holtec are closing the certification and deployment gap. Returns are deeply negative with no free cash flow, so there is no earnings base to defend.

Moat trend: stable to eroding — the certification advantage erodes as peers advance through NRC licensing and actually break ground. TVA's first U.S. SMR site at Clinch River uses GE-Hitachi's BWRX-300, not NuScale's design.

Secular threat: the secular tailwind — AI capital-spending and data-center power demand — is the bull case, not a threat. Terminal value is not structurally impaired by a secular force; the risk is execution, dilution, and timing. Secular threat is manageable.

Management credibility 2 of 5; trajectory turning; moat narrow but real; moat trend eroding; secular threat manageable.
skeptic_kill_fact: No single fact fully breaks the bear case — the bear case is the surviving view here — but the bull-leaning load-bearing claims weaken on primary-source check. Two corrections materially worsen value capture. First, the share-count anchor used in the debate is wrong: total shares outstanding are approximately 343.1 million (323.7 million Class A plus 19.4 million Class B as of March 31, 2026 per the 10-Q), not the 365 million-plus cited, so the per-share cash-floor math is slightly off. Second, and far more important, the ENTRA1 relationship is a net cash outflow for NuScale, not a contracted inflow: NuScale paid ENTRA1 approximately $495 million in Q3 2025 (general and administrative expenses ballooned roughly 3,000% to $519 million in that quarter), and total milestone payments to ENTRA1 could exceed $3 billion based on the CEO's own figures. This is the subject of an active securities-fraud class action (Truedson v. NuScale, U.S. District Court for the District of Oregon, case number 3:26-cv-00328, class period May 13, 2025 to November 6, 2025, lead-plaintiff deadline April 20, 2026) that the debate did not capture. The competitive-vulnerability point also verifies: TVA's first U.S. SMR at Clinch River is GE-Hitachi's BWRX-300, with Bechtel selected as builder, not NuScale. Net: the soft catalyst, third-party structure, pay-the-intermediary economics, and active securities litigation all support the pass-for-now verdict and a lower allocation within the basket, but no verified fact converts the thesis from a credible speculative option to outright dead.
skeptic_corrections: 1) Share count: use approximately 343 million total (323.7 million Class A plus 19.4 million Class B, from the 10-Q filed for the period ended March 31, 2026), not 365 million-plus. This modestly raises the per-share cash floor from roughly $3.30 toward roughly $3.40 to $3.50 at approximately $1.0 billion in liquidity. 2) Liquidity is approximately $1.0 billion at Q1 2026 ($341.1 million cash plus $549.0 million short-term investments), not the roughly $1.2 billion by May figure the bull case cites. Verify any $1.2 billion figure before relying on it. 3) The net loss attributable to Class A shareholders in Q1 2026 was $44.0 million ($0.14 per share); the debate's $46.7 million is the wrong line item. 4) The most important undisclosed disqualifier: the ENTRA1 economics flow out of NuScale, not in. NuScale paid ENTRA1 approximately $495 million in Q3 2025, and the CEO's own figures imply total milestone payments to ENTRA1 could exceed $3 billion. An active securities-fraud class action covers exactly this disclosure gap. Value capture is not merely 'a derivative of ENTRA1's structure' as the debate frames it — NuScale is the payer, not the contracted payee, until an equipment-supply contract reverses the cash flow.
skeptic_evidence: NuScale 8-K/Q1-2026 results (filed 2026-05-07, SEC EDGAR CIK 0001822966): Q1-26 revenue $0.565M vs $13.4M PY; net loss to Class A $44.0M ($0.14/sh); operating loss $57.5M; operating cash outflow $314.7M; liquidity ~$1.0B ($341.1M cash + $549.0M short-term investments).
NuScale 10-Q for period ended 2026-03-31 (SEC EDGAR, smr-20260331.htm): 323,741,458 Class A + 19,375,371 Class B shares outstanding (~343.1M total) — refutes the '365M+' share count.

World Nuclear News, 'TVA submits first US BWRX-300 construction application' (2025/2026): TVA's first US SMR at Clinch River is GE-Hitachi BWRX-300 with Bechtel as builder — confirms NuScale is NOT TVA's first deployed reactor.

PRNewswire / Kessler Topaz, 'NuScale Power Corporation (SMR) Securities Fraud Class Action' (lead-plaintiff deadline 2026-04-20; Truedson v. NuScale, D.Or. 3:26-cv-00328): alleges Q3-25 G&A rose ~3,000% to $519M due to a ~$495M payment to ENTRA1; CEO acknowledged milestone payments could exceed $3B — confirms ENTRA1 cash flows OUT of NuScale and the catalyst carries active litigation risk.

scale.role: NuScale SMR developer; binary on the RoPower/PPA pipeline, ENTRA1 cash flows OUT.
scale.rationale: Binary with a fat left tail and low franchise centrality (~20): base ~$7 is ~40% below spot, $3.3-3.5 cash floor if the PPA slips. Skeptic verdict CONFIRMED_WITH_CORRECTIONS, and the corrections WORSEN it materially — the ENTRA1 economics flow OUT of NuScale (paid ~$495M Q3-25, potential >$3B milestone payments), there is an active securities-fraud class action, and TVA's first US SMR is GE-Hitachi's BWRX-300, not NuScale — a load-bearing haircut that confirms PASS.
scale.demand_durability: Low — binary on a single pipeline; NuScale is the payer to ENTRA1, not yet the contracted payee.
